engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: One move from winning — then you looked away from your king
mistakes.explanation: You were up a clean piece with a crushing +5.7. Black's only real attacker is the bishop on f6, shielding your invasion and eyeing your king. 20.Bxf6+ trades it off with check and leaves you a piece up with a quiet position — 57% of players at your level find it. Instead 20.Bh6 sidestepped and swung the door open: Black's rooks and queen pour down the g- and h-files and there is now a forced mate (…Rxg2+, …Qh3, …Qxh2#). The winning move wasn't a deep tactic — it just needed the question “is my own king safe?” before admiring the extra material.

takeaways.lesson: When you're winning, defend before you decorate.
takeaways.detail: In any position you think is won, spend five seconds on one question before moving: can my opponent give a check or a capture near my king next move? Deal with that first.

takeaways.lesson: Trade off the one piece doing the attacking.
takeaways.detail: When a single enemy piece carries the whole attack (here the f6 bishop), hunt for a way to exchange it — a capture with check like Bxf6+ is the dream version.

mistakes.title: Developing a piece while the mate lands next move
mistakes.explanation: Still +6.7 on the board, but the attack you invited two moves ago is now one move from mating you: …Rxg2+ followed by …Qh3 and …Qxh2# is coming. 23.Nb5 reaches for more — a knight lunge at your extra queenside pawns — while the fire is in your own house. The engine's 23.Kh1 tucks the king away and there is still nothing Black can do; you stay up a piece. Only 12% of players your level find Kh1 here, which is the real tell: once the attack is on, defending your king has to jump the queue over every ambitious idea on the far side of the board.

drillLinks.label: Defending against an attack — Lichess blog

takeaways.lesson: A threat against your king outranks a plan against theirs.
takeaways.detail: When you spot an attack forming near your own king, stop generating new ideas until you've answered it. Ambition on one wing means nothing if you're mated on the other.

takeaways.lesson: Give the king an escape square early.
takeaways.detail: Moves like Kh1 / g3 that create luft or step off a vulnerable diagonal are cheap insurance — play them the moment enemy heavy pieces point at your castled position, not after.

mistakes.title: A free pawn with check — that pried open your own king
mistakes.explanation: 19.Bxg5+ is the most human move in the position — 56% of players at your level grab it — but taking on g5 tears open the very g-file Black's rook later mates on. You were +7.3; there was no rush. The engine's cool 19.c5 keeps the center clamped, and even the natural 19.Qxa6 (which 19% of your peers would pick) grabs a pawn on the safe side of the board and holds the whole edge. This is a knowledge gap, not a blunder: the fix is a rule you can carry into every game — don't open lines next to your own king for a pawn.

takeaways.lesson: Don't rip open a file next to your own king for a pawn.
takeaways.detail: Before a capture near your king, picture the file or diagonal it opens. If an enemy rook or queen would then stare at your king, the pawn isn't worth it — especially when you're already winning.

takeaways.lesson: When you're up material, simplify and shut lines, don't open them.
takeaways.detail: A big lead is converted by keeping the position calm. Prefer moves that trade attackers or keep files closed over moves that grab a little more and invite complications.

mistakes.title: The same pawn grab that was safe on move 19 was fatal on move 21
mistakes.explanation: Two moves earlier, taking on a6 was fine. Now it loses the game. Black has spent the interval building — …g5, …Bf6, …Rf7 — and your king has no defenders, so 21.Qxa6 sends your last active piece to the far corner while the storm breaks. Your evaluation collapses from +5 to dead level (winning chances 87% → 50%). The engine's 21.g3 and the human-simple 21.Kh1 both just look after the king — and you're still winning. The queen had already eaten five pawns; the sixth cost you the game. When your opponent's pieces are massing on your king, call the queen home to help defend instead of sending it further away.

takeaways.lesson: Count your attackers and defenders around your king before grabbing material.
takeaways.detail: Ask literally: how many enemy pieces are aimed at my king, and how many of mine defend it? If they outnumber you, the next move is a defender, not a capture.

takeaways.lesson: An offside queen is a missing defender.
takeaways.detail: A queen munching pawns on the a-file isn't helping your king. When an attack is brewing, the fastest defensive resource is often to bring the queen back toward home.

highlights.note: The engine's top choice and the move that broke the game open: 6.d5 hit the c6 knight and, after the trade on f3, left it stranded — a central break only 41% of players your level find here. Reading the moment a pawn push wins material is exactly the timing skill worth keeping.

Quiet, patient, and found by just 8% of players at your level: 10.c4 clamps the queenside and hands Black no counterplay while you finish developing. This is the calm, space-gaining instinct the middlegame collapse later abandoned — trust it more often.

openingReport.note: You followed the Ponziani (3.c3) out to move 3 and were on your own by move 5. The first real slip came at move 8: 8.Bd3 retreated the attacked bishop when 8.dxc6 simply won the stranded c6 knight. After 8.Bd3 Nce7 Black rescued the piece and your edge fell from +5 to +1 (winning chances 87% → 60%).
